entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud add-in for Microsoft Excel: Innovation, adoption and AI vision
entry.content:
Excel isn't going anywhere. It's getting smarter.

Join our partner exclusive webinar to discover what's new with the SAP Analytics Cloud add-in for Microsoft Excel and why more customers are making it part of their analytics and planning strategy.

We'll cover adoption trends, the features customers use most, and practical lessons from real implementations. You'll also get a first look at the latest H1 2026 innovations and hear how AI is influencing the product vision for the year ahead.

Whether you're in sales, pre-sales, consulting, implementation, or customer advisory, you'll leave with insights you can apply in your next customer conversation.

Don't miss this opportunity to stay current, sharpen your expertise, and help customers get more value from their data.

Thank you for your detailed explanation and recommendations.

We understand that the Legacy Main Number - Subnumber field is intended only for legacy asset migration and is not designed for ongoing maintenance.

Based on your advice, we will first evaluate whether the Inventory Number field can be used to store the external fixed asset management number. If it does not meet our business requirements, we will also consider creating a custom field.

Thank you for your support.

Best regards,

entry.title: Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere using T006 (LB to KG Example)
entry.content:
Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere Using T006/T006D – LB to KG Example

Introduction:

During a recent SAP Datasphere reporting requirement, I needed to present quantities in a consistent unit of measure even though the source data was stored in different units. Rather than implementing custom conversion logic, I explored how SAP standard unit conversion tables could be leveraged through the CONVERT_UNIT function.

 

In SAP Datasphere, data frequently originates from multiple source systems that use different units of measure. To ensure accurate analytics and reporting, these quantities often need to be converted into a common business unit. In this example, the requirement is to convert quantities from LB to KG using SAP standard unit conversion logic.

 

This blog explains the SAP standard tables involved in unit conversion, the implementation approach in SAP Datasphere, the validation process, and the key lessons learned during the exercise.

 

Business Use Case:

Consider a manufacturing company that receives material quantities from suppliers in LB, while corporate reporting standards require all quantities to be reported in KG.

Without a standard conversion mechanism, reports from different locations become difficult to compare. SAP Datasphere's unit conversion capability helps standardize these quantities using SAP-maintained conversion factors and dimensions.

For example:

  • Source system stores quantity as 568 LB
  • Business users want reporting output in KG
  • Manual conversion in every report can lead to inconsistency
  • A centralized conversion logic in SAP Datasphere gives consistent and reusable results

This type of conversion is commonly needed in:

  • Inventory reporting
  • Procurement analytics
  • Material movement reporting
  • Cross-country operational reporting
  • Consolidated quantity reporting across multiple source systems

 

Prerequisites to understand:

Before implementing unit conversion in SAP Datasphere, it is important to understand the SAP standard tables used for unit conversion.

The two primary SAP standard tables involved in unit conversion are:

  • T006D – Dimension table
  • T006 – Unit of measurement table

Because these tables are maintained centrally within SAP, they provide a consistent and trusted source of conversion factors across applications and reporting scenarios.

 

T006D (Dimensions):

This table stores unit dimensions. DIMID is a primary key in this table, and it represents the ‘Dimension’, and MSSIE stores one unique SI/base unit for that dimension.

For example, for the dimension MASS, the SI/base unit is KG.

So, when we convert from LB to KG, both units belong to the same dimension: MASS.

This is important because unit conversion should happen only between units that belong to the same logical dimension.

 

Once the dimension and base unit have been identified in T006D, the next step is to understand how individual units within that dimension are maintained in T006.

 

T006 (Units of Measurement):

T006 stores the units of measurement and their conversion factors.

For the MASS dimension, units such as G, KG, OZ, LB, and TON can be maintained.

 

How to Implement Unit Conversion in Datasphere:

  1. Create a simple Fact View on the source table, for example ACDOCA, and enable the required fields.  In my current implementation, the source quantity field is MSL and the source unit field is RUNIT.
  2. Create 2 calculated columns in the ‘Formula’ node
  • MSL_Converted – to convert quantity
  • RUNIT_Converted – to display the converted target unit

 

 

Use the CONVERT_UNIT function to convert the source quantity from LB to KG.

In this example, the target unit is hardcoded as KG for simplicity.

However, this logic is suitable only for a simple demo scenario. In real projects, avoid hardcoding too many unit mappings in calculated columns. Instead, it is better to use an Input Parameter for the target unit, if business users need flexibility to convert quantities into different units such as KG, G, or TON

 

 

To show the converted unit in the output, create another calculated column.

 

This makes the output easier for business users to understand because the converted quantity and converted unit are shown together.

 

After deploying the view, perform a data preview and validate the converted result.

Manual Data Validation:

 

 

For the LB unit, the important fields used in this example are:

Field

Meaning

Value in this example

MSEHI

Unit of measure

LB

DIMID

Dimension

MASS

ZAEHL

Numerator

453592

NENNR

Denominator

1000000

EXP10

Power of 10 scaling factor

0

 

Here Quantity in Source Unit (ACDOCA-MSL) = 568

                                                           Source Unit = LB

                                                            Target Unit = KG

From T006:

ZAEHL = 453592

NENNR = 1000000

EXP10 = 0

 

 

The LB to KG conversion factor is derived as:

 

Quantity in Target Unit = Quantity in Source Unit * (ZAEHL / NENNR)

Quantity in Target Unit   = 568*(453592/1000000)

Quantity in Target Unit = 568 *(0.453592)

Quantity in Target Unit = 257.640256 KG

This matches the value returned by the CONVERT_UNIT function, so the conversion is verified and correct.

Note: SAP also stores an additional field called EXP10 (exponent), which is used for scaling in some unit conversions.
In this example, EXP10 = 0, so it does not impact the calculation.

 

Area

What to Check

Why It Matters

Source unit

Ensure the source unit value exists in T006

If the source unit is missing or invalid, conversion can fail

Dimension

Ensure source and target units belong to the same dimension

LB and KG belong to MASS, so conversion is valid

Conversion factor

Validate ZAEHL, NENNR, and EXP10

These fields determine the actual conversion factor

Target unit

Avoid unnecessary hardcoding

Hardcoding is okay for demos, but parameters are better for reusable models

Error handling

Use appropriate error handling based on requirement

fail on error helps identify missing or incorrect unit conversion data early

Validation

Always compare Datasphere output with manual calculation

This builds confidence before using the logic in reports

 

When Is Unit Conversion Needed?

Unit conversion is useful when the same business measure is captured in different units across systems or processes.

Some common scenarios are:

  • Source data stores quantity in vendor-specific units
  • Reporting requires a standard unit across countries or plants
  • Different SAP or non-SAP systems send different units
  • Planning and analytics require normalized quantities
  • Business users compare material quantities across regions

For example, if one plant stores inventory in LB and another plant reports in KG, the analytical output becomes inconsistent unless the quantities are converted into a common unit.

 

Common Pitfall:

One common issue is attempting to convert between units that belong to different dimensions. For example, SAP will not perform a valid conversion between MASS and LENGTH dimensions because their measurement contexts are unrelated. Therefore, validating the DIMID relationship in T006D should be one of the first troubleshooting steps when conversion results are unexpected.

Another common issue is hardcoding conversion factors instead of using SAP standard conversion logic. While manual calculations may work for simple examples, they can become difficult to maintain when conversion definitions change or additional units need to be supported.

 

Lessons Learned:

During this implementation, I observed the following:

  • Always verify that source and target units belong to the same dimension in T006D.
  • Validate Datasphere results using T006 conversion factors before deploying to production.
  • INPUT PARAMETERS provide more flexibility than hardcoded target units.
  • EXP10 should be reviewed for conversions involving scaling factors.
  • CONVERT_UNIT is preferable to manual calculations because it leverages SAP standard conversion logic.

 

Conclusion:

In this exercise, I used SAP standard tables T006 and T006D together with the CONVERT_UNIT function to convert quantities from LB to KG in SAP Datasphere.

 

The converted value was validated against SAP-maintained conversion factors, confirming the accuracy of the result. By relying on SAP's standard conversion framework instead of custom formulas, organizations can build more scalable, reusable, and reliable reporting solutions when working with multiple units of measure.

 

I hope this example helps you understand not only how unit conversion works in SAP Datasphere, but also how SAP standard tables can be used to validate and troubleshoot conversion results effectively.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What's New as of 2026-07-06?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

Welcome to this month's What's New blog post for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). We're delighted to present the latest improvements and innovations that continue to enhance the platform, helping you work more efficiently while delivering a smoother and more intuitive experience.

Let's explore what's new! 🚀

Process Scheduler | Environment Lifecycle Management

Previously, recurring Process Scheduler jobs were not part of the environment lifecycle. Every environment copy or import meant recreating all job definitions manually in the target environment, and deleting an environment left orphaned jobs behind in the Process Scheduler.

With this update, recurring Process Scheduler jobs are now fully integrated across four environment operations: Copy, Export, Import, and Deletion. Job definitions travel with their environment and are automatically removed when the environment is deleted. Copy and import operations include an opt-in checkbox to include recurring jobs, and all operations are tracked in the Application Monitor.

COPY:

IMPORT:

For a full walkthrough of each operation, see our dedicated blog post. For more information, refer to the Process Scheduler documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

Content Management and Application Monitor | Improved Logs for Environment Import

We've improved the logging for environment imports in Content Management. The import log entry now includes the original imported file name, giving you better visibility and traceability during the import process.

What changed?

Previously, when an environment import was initiated, the Application Monitor log showed:

"Model Import started for Environment=DSE, Version=0010"

With this improvement, the log now displays: 

"Model Import started for Environment=DSE, Version=0010 based on the original content DSE-0001-20260527T132918847Z.zip" 



Why does this matter? 

When managing multiple imports — especially in automated TMS (Transport Management System) deployments — it was difficult to trace back which specific file initiated a particular import. The updated log entry now makes it easy to:

  1. Identify the exact source file of any import.
  2. Audit and troubleshoot import activities more efficiently.
  3. Correlate Application Monitor entries with the files used in your deployment pipeline.

Removal of the Optional Feature for the Python Executor

The Optional Feature for the Python Executor has been removed from the Settings screen. The Python 3.13 implementation is now the permanent and only supported executor for the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) Python function type in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM).

Previously, administrators could activate  the new Python executor and switch back to the previous implementation if required. As the migration to Python 3.13 has been completed, this configuration option is no longer available, and all RFA Python functions now run using the latest implementation by default.

What Changed?

  • The use New Remote Function Adapter Python Implementation toggle has been removed from Administration > Settings > Optional Features.
  • Python 3.13 is now the permanent and only supported Python executor.
  • Administrators no longer need to configure the Python executor version.

Benefits:

  • Simplifies system administration by removing an obsolete configuration option.
  • Ensures a consistent Python execution environment across all tenants.
  • Eliminates the possibility of reverting to the legacy Python executor, providing a single supported runtime for all RFA Python executions.

Modeling | Partitioning Function: Dynamic Packaging Type

The Partitioning function now supports a new Packaging Type dropdown, added directly after the Run Mode field in the properties panel. This lets you choose whether partition ranges are fixed at design time or calculated at runtime from the actual Input function data.

  • Static (default): Ranges are defined at design time as inner nodes in the Partitioning function. No change to existing models are required.
  • Dynamic: Ranges are calculated at runtime from the Input function entries. Any existing Range nodes are removed when a Static Partitioning is updated to Dynamic.

When Dynamic is selected, Run Mode values PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, and SUXP are not available, and Range Levels and Destinations are not supported.



The Field property has been renamed to Packaging Field and repositioned directly after Packaging Type. Multiple Partitioning functions can reference the same Packaging Field as long as each uses a different Run Mode or Packaging Type.


For more details about the Dynamic Packaging Partitioning, you may check SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model - Introducing Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning.

Joule | AI Enhancements

Joule now supports uploading .txt and .docx files as additional context for your conversations, expanding the range of file formats available for upload.


Report Management | Create, Rewrite, or Delete Report Sections

You can now use Joule in Act mode to create new text sections, rewrite existing ones, or delete sections from your report directly through the Joule conversation panel — without manually editing the report.

Previously, report text could only be modified by entering Edit mode manually. The Inline Report Editing feature was introduced, allowing you to rewrite or improve text directly within your report.

With this update, you can instruct Joule conversationally to shape your report content for a single section, or multiple sections in a single prompt.

To get started, open the Joule panel in the Report Management application, choose Act to switch to Act mode, and type your instruction referring to the target section by its title.

The following actions are supported:

Action

Example Prompt

Rewrite an existing section    

"Please rewrite the Processes and Activities section giving it more of the business touch."

Add a new text section

"Add a new Key Risks and Mitigations section after the Summary section."

Delete a text section

"Delete the Introduction section."

Joule applies non-destructive actions (create, rewrite) immediately and automatically refreshes the report screen — the requested modification is applied and displayed in the report without any manual refresh needed.

For destructive actions such as deletion, Joule asks for your confirmation before proceeding — choose Approve to apply or Reject to cancel.



The following notes apply:

  • If you add a new section without specifying a position, Joule adds it at the end of the report.
  • Deleted sections cannot be recovered.
  • For rewritten sections, you can ask Joule to restore the previous content based on the conversation context.
  • If you are in Edit mode with unsaved changes, save or discard them first — Joule will prompt you if needed.

For more information, refer to the relevant pages on our SAP Help Portal: Create, Rewrite, or Delete Report Sections | Prompting Recommendations | Inline Report Editing

Modeling Screen | Remote Function Adapter: General Function

A new function type is now available in the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) palette in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model: the General Function.


The General Function connects SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model to a custom Function Module on your On-Premise SAP system through an RFC destination configured in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This allows existing On-Premise ABAP logic — written as a BAPI wrapper — to be called directly from your calculation model, with input data passed in through IT_DATA and results returned through ET_DATA into the result table. No rebuilding of On-Premise logic in the Cloud is required.

To use the General Function, the following must be in place:

  1. A custom Function Module on your On-Premise system, configured as Remote-Enabled (SE37) and structured with the required IT_DATA, ET_DATA, and ET_MESSAGE parameters.
  2. An RFC Destination in SAP BTP Destination Service, with the On-Premise system mapped via SAP Cloud Connector.



Once configured, the General Function can be run through all standard SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model run entry points, including direct Run, Run with Parameters and Selections, Process Template, Process Instance, and Process Scheduler.

For a full walkthrough of configuration, BAPI compatibility requirements, and end-to-end verification, see our dedicated blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model – Introducing the Remote Function Adapter: General Function.

File Uploads | Enhanced File Validation Across Applications

File upload and import operations across SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model now include enhanced security validations, providing stronger protection against malformed, misnamed, or potentially malicious files.

Previously, uploads were protected by malware scanning only. With this update, additional validation checks are applied automatically during every file upload or import operation including real file type detection (verifying the actual file content, not just the extension), file name validation, and improved handling of archive files.

The following applications are affected:

Application

Use Case

Supported Formats

Data Management

Upload data to Model Tables and Queries

CSV, XLSX

Content Management

Upload / import environments

ZIP

Process Management

Mass upload of process instance settings

XLSX

Report Management

Upload images to Report panels

BMP, JPEG, JPG, GIF, PNG, TIFF, WEBP

Joule

Attach files to Joule conversations

CSV, XLSX, PDF

No action is required on your side as the validations run automatically. Each step of the file upload process is logged, and the system displays an appropriate message to inform you of the outcome whether the upload was successful, or the file was rejected due to a validation or security scanning failure.

For more information, refer to our SAP Help Portal: Secure File Handling  

That's all for this update!

We hope these enhancements help further improve your experience with SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model. Stay tuned for more updates and innovations in the coming months.

Until next time! 😊

entry.title: Work Zone advanced Edition cdm_export_service
entry.content:
We would like to extract information from the Content Cards in BTP Work Zone Advanced Edition via cdm_export_service/v1/export/Entities. For example, we are trying to download the ZIP file via the portal service using this body:
{
‘entityRefs’: [
{ ‘ID’: ‘dws-widget-mr923ntn’, “Type”: ‘catalog’ }
]
}
Unfortunately, we only receive an Error.json file containing the following information:
[{‘detailedErrorID’:301,‘errorType’:‘General’,‘errorMessage’:‘Request failed with status code 500’,‘agentId’:‘content-repository’,“correlationId”:‘c9d732e3-0d30-42ae-5d0a-1cbb142679d8’}]
Are we doing something wrong, or is this simply not possible in the Advanced Edition?
Thanks for your help.
Jens

Translated with DeepL.com (free version)

entry.title: SAP Academic Community Conference NA 2026 - Digital Hub
entry.content:
We are excited to welcome you to the SAP ACC NA Digital Hub - your dedicated space to connect, collaborate, and continue the conversation throughout and beyond the conference.

This Digital Hub is designed to help you get the most out of your SAP ACC NA experience by providing a place where attendees, speakers, and SAP experts can engage, share insights, and learn from one another.

How to Connect and Engage

We encourage you to use this space to:

  • Ask follow-up questions about conference sessions and topics
  • Continue discussions with speakers and fellow participants
  • Share your experiences, ideas, and best practices
  • Exchange teaching and learning insights with peers

Don't Miss Any Discussions – Subscribe to the Label

To stay informed about all SAP ACC NA Digital Hub activities, we recommend that you:

Subscribe to the label "SAP ACC NA Digital Hub"

 

By subscribing, you'll receive notifications whenever new discussions and content are published under this label.

Creating a New Discussion

When posting a new discussion related to SAP ACC NA, please make sure to:

Add the label "SAP ACC NA Digital Hub" to your post

Using the label helps community members easily find all conference-related discussions in one place and ensures your post reaches others who are following the Digital Hub.

Explore and Contribute

The Digital Hub is designed to support ongoing collaboration within our community. Whether you want to reconnect with someone you met during the conference, seek advice from experts, or continue exploring a topic that inspired you, this is the place to do it.

Earn Your SAP ACC Digital Badge!

Don't forget that you can also earn your SAP ACC Digital Badge. Simply visit the dedicated blog post and leave a reply indicating whether you participated as an attendee or a speaker.

We look forward to your questions, ideas, and discussions. Let's make this a collaborative, engaging, and insightful experience for everyone!

And if not done yet, remember to join the SAP University Alliances program group to get the most out of your experience!

entry.title: A complete guide: How the BTP upgrade changes the supplier experience on SAP Business Network
entry.content:
Platform upgrades only matter if they make work easier and improve how organizations do business. And that’s what is behind SAP Business Network’s move to SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).

The transition may sound like a back-end technical change, but it brings a faster, more reliable experience, fewer manual tasks, and new AI capabilities for suppliers – all while preserving the accounts, documents, integrations, and buyer relationships they rely on every day.

Continue transacting without disruption

If you're already doing business on the network, the good news is that the BTP upgrade happens behind the scenes, without disrupting your day-to-day operations.

Your supplier account, ANID, catalogs, documents, buyer relationships, and existing integrations – including portal, cXML, EDI, and APIs – will remain fully intact. You can log in and continue managing orders, catalogs, shipments, and invoices as you do today. SAP migrates documents, configurations, business rules, and integrations together, so you don’t need to reimplement existing buyer connections or pause transactions.

What changes is the experience around those tasks. By running on SAP BTP, SAP Business Network becomes easier to use, more scalable, and better equipped to support automation and future SAP innovations.

Prepare for the migration with a few simple steps

  • Continue using your SAP Business Network account as usual
  • No reimplementation of existing buyer connections or integrations is required
  • Review SAP communications regarding migration timing and new features
  • Perform routine validation testing if you use complex integrations
  • Familiarize yourself with new capabilities such as BNOIDs and AI capabilities as they become available

Identify the right business entities and reduce routing errors 

As part of the upgrade, you will see a new identifier in your profile called a BNOID (Business Network Organization ID). This is a more precise ID for a legal entity, subsidiary, brand, or business unit within your company. ANIDs continue to identify your company’s account on SAP Business Network and remain in place during the transition to BTP, but over time BNOIDs will become the primary ID to help you and your buyers connect at a deeper organizational level with less manual routing and less confusion.

Complete tasks faster with a more intuitive interface

One of the first improvements you’ll notice following the BTP upgrade is the refreshed user interface, which uses SAP Fiori. This gives you a cleaner and more consistent way to work across SAP Business Network, as well as other SAP applications. You get:

  • Clearer pages for purchase orders, invoices, rules, and notifications
  • Better filtering to find the right documents faster
  • Side-panel views that let you review line details without losing your place
  • Fewer clicks to where you need to go 
  • A more familiar SAP experience if you already use other SAP tools

We know suppliers can spend significant time on repetitive tasks like checking orders, fixing invoice errors, updating content, and answering buyer requests. These front-end improvements help reduce that effort. According to SAP internal benchmarking, guided navigation has been shown to deliver 45% faster document submissions, 24% fewer first-time entry errors, 70% better data accuracy, and 41% fewer process errors.


Connect with the right buyer entities from the start

Onboarding often starts with repeated requests, manual follow-ups, and buyer confusion over the correct legal entity. With the BTP update, SAP Business Network gives your buyers a unified, verified view of trading partners so they can select the right legal entity from the start. 

For instance, if you supply multiple subsidiaries of a large buyer, they can now select the correct entity immediately, rather than inviting a generic parent company and sorting out the details after the buying process has started. You can accept the invitation, map the right locations and users, and begin transacting sooner.

Speed up onboarding and start transacting sooner

The BTP upgrade also introduces the Supplier Onboarding Agent, which automates parts of the onboarding process, so you receive purchase orders and get paid faster. You do not need to enable it yourself; it works through buyer invites and guided steps when a buyer using the agent on BTP connects with you. SAP data shows that it can improve onboarding cycle time by 50% and reduce manual supplier identification, extraction, and follow-up work by 75%.

Keep your current integrations while improving performance and reliability 

Your existing portal, cXML, EDI, and API integrations will continue to work without disruption throughout the move to BTP. Most suppliers can validate important processes with routine checks, while those with more complex integrations can coordinate testing with their buyer and SAP teams. In most cases, you’ll continue transacting without reimplementing existing integrations.

The reliability behind those integrations is what’s different. SAP reports 54% faster response times for users and APIs and 10x processing capacity to handle spikes and growth. For you, that means:

  • Fewer integration-related slowdowns
  • More consistent document status updates
  • Better handling during volume spikes
  • Less effort chasing errors across systems

See the full structure of every buyer relationship before you transact

Another benefit of the move to BTP is greater visibility into the organizations you do business with.

In the past, you may have seen only a single buyer account or contact, making it difficult to identify the correct legal entity for invoicing, determine whether another subsidiary was already connected, or understand the full scope of your relationship with that buyer.

With the Trading Partner Graph on BTP, you get a clearer view of verified buyer organization structures and the full context of your relationship with each customer. This helps you connect and transact with the correct entities more easily, while protecting confidential business information.

This means you can:

  • Route documents to the right legal entity more confidently
  • Understand parent and child relationships more clearly
  • Identify overlaps or gaps in where you already do business

An example: You receive an order from “Global Manufacturing Group.” The Trading Partner Graph shows that the actual ordering entity is “GMG Europe GmbH,” not the parent company. You send the order confirmation, advanced shipping notice, and invoice to the correct entity the first time. That reduces the risk of invoice rejection and speeds up the payment.

Work more efficiently with built-in AI capabilities

As part of SAP’s broader strategy to embed AI across its business applications, the BTP upgrade creates the foundation for bringing intelligent, in-context support directly into SAP Business Network. That means AI becomes a natural part of the platform, and the workflows you already use every day, not a separate tool you need to find, launch, or add on top. For you, this results in faster task completion, less manual work, and more efficient issue resolution without switching between systems.

Examples include:

  • Invoice rejection classification – helps you understand why invoices were rejected, identify recurring issues, and resubmit.
  • Product content author – generates draft product descriptions and summaries for catalog items.
  • Lead response author – helps you respond to Discovery leads more quickly and effectively.
  • Profile keyword generator – suggests keywords that can improve your visibility in buyer searches.
  • Support and navigation assistance – helps you find the right screens, understand next steps, and resolve issues more efficiently.

By automating repetitive tasks and providing guidance, these capabilities help you spend reduce time spent on administrative work and spend more on growing your business.

The real value of the BTP upgrade for suppliers

The move to SAP BTP provides you with three major benefits:

  • Continuity: Existing accounts, documents, and integrations continue to work
  • Efficiency: A modern interface, better entity management, and streamlined onboarding reduce manual effort
  • AI support: AI capabilities and a scalable platform prepare you for future SAP innovations

The value of the SAP BTP upgrade is not the platform change itself, but what it makes easier in your daily work. SAP Business Network becomes simpler to use, more connected, and more dependable.

Independent analyst perspective

To learn how industry analysts view the evolution of SAP Business Network, read the Ardent Partners brief, "The Fabric of Business: SAP Business Network and the AI-First Enterprise" (May 2026).

Frequently asked questions

My supplier account hasn’t been migrated – when will this happen?

Your timeline will depend on your account type. SAP is rolling out the new interface and capabilities in phases throughout 2026. Newly registered accounts are already using the refreshed user interface. For existing accounts, migration timing will vary, and suppliers using Supply Chain Collaboration will move according to a separate schedule. In all cases, you’ll receive an email notification 30 days before your migration date. Watch out for the subject line “Supplier Network Account Upgrade Notification” in the inbox associated with your supplier account.

I’m already on the upgraded SAP Business Network – where can I find more hands-on information about the changes?

All suppliers can access the dedicated SAP Business Network on BTP site, which hosts everything new and existing suppliers need to know about the migration. Watch the on-demand webinar for a tour of the new supplier experience or go directly to the how-to demo videos.

Do I need to do anything with my BNOID right now?

No immediate action is required. Your BNOID is assigned automatically, and your operations continue unchanged with your ANID. For more information, you can review the BTP FAQ document.

How do I access Joule and AI capabilities on SAP Business Network?

Access to Joule and other AI capabilities on SAP Business Network depends on several factors, including your BTP migration wave, feature enablement, region, and user role. As SAP Business Network continues its move to SAP BTP, these AI capabilities are actively being developed and rolled out in phases. That means some features may already be available in your environment, while others are still being introduced or expanded over time.

entry.title: Re: How to create completed Supplier data by BO Interface
entry.content:
Hello Neel, 

Good Day! 

While looking into the details of the BO  - I_BUSINESSPARTNERTP_3, We could see that, this supports the General Business partner details (Address, Bank Details, Tax Details etc.) and doesn’t support the Company Codes or Purchasing org related data for the tabs highlighted. 

As per the SAP Note 3655149 ,  

  • The Business Partner object I_BUSINESSPARTNERTP_3 currently supports only Business Partner datasets (such as General Data, Address, Bank, Industry, etc.) and does not support creating or extending Customer or Supplier roles. 
  • To create or extend the Business Partner with Customer or Supplier roles, use the API_BUSINESS_PARTNER.  

So, the recommended solution would be to use the Odata service API_BUSINESS_PARTNER. 

The details of the service can be found in the following link. 

https://api.sap.com/api/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER/overview 

 Thanks, 

Maciej

entry.title: Re: Unable to Set Payment Terms and Payment Method for Ve...
entry.content:
Dear @StrOk,

The Clear Outgoing Payments app supports residual item creation by entering a residual amount and assigning reason code/reference information. SAP KBA 3399348 confirms that a residual item clears the original item and creates a new open item referencing the original invoice

For Payment Terms the relevant configuration area is the customer/supplier tolerance configuration. In Public Cloud, this is maintained in SSCUI 102923 - Define Tolerances for Customers and Suppliers. SAP KBAs 3002697 and 3539821 confirm this SSCUI for customer/supplier payment difference tolerance settings in Public Cloud. Therefore the available residual item payment term behavior should be checked and maintained there, depending on the fields available in the customer configuration environment

For Payment Method, I have not found a SAP documented setting in F1367 or in the residual-item tolerance configuration that allows a payment method to be assigned directly to the newly created vendor residual item during posting. Therefore, if the generated vendor residual item has a blank Payment Method this is consistent with the standard behavior found in Public Cloud

If a Payment Method is required on the generated supplier open item the practical standard option is to maintain it afterwards in Manage Supplier Line Items, if the field is editable for that item. SAP KBA 3043534 confirms that Payment Method can be changed via Manage Supplier Line Items -> Edit Line Items, subject to standard restrictions

Recommended approach:

  1. Check SSCUI 102923 – Define Tolerances for Customers and Suppliers for the relevant customer/supplier tolerance group
  2. Check whether the required residual-item payment term behavior can be maintained there
  3. Assign the relevant tolerance group to the business partner
  4. Post the clearing again in Clear Outgoing Payments (F1367)
  5. If a Payment Method is required on the generated vendor residual open item, maintain it afterwards in Manage Supplier Line Items, if editable
  6. If the business requirement is to enter or automatically derive a specific Payment Method directly during residual item creation in F1367, submit this as an improvement request via SAP Customer Influence

More details below:

SAP Help – Clear Outgoing Payments

SAP KBA 3399348 – Partial payment and residual payment in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP KBA 3002697 – How to use absolute amounts only for maximum allowable revenues from payment differences

SAP KBA 3539821 – Permitted Payment Differences set up in Activity “Define Tolerances for Customers and Suppliers”

SAP KBA 3043534 – Payment method or baseline date cannot be changed in Manage Supplier Line Items app

SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Building a RAP Application with External SAP HANA Cloud using CDS External Entities – Part 1
entry.content:
Why External Entities?

CDS external entities provide a modern way to handle secondary database connections in ABAP Cloud. They allow applications to retrieve and modify data from other databases using SQL. Sounds interesting? This blog post covers how to create a RAP application in SAP BTP ABAP Environment  with a secondary HANA database connection.

 

Business scenario:


Many organizations already have business data stored in a separate SAP HANA Cloud database and want to build modern RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) applications on SAP BTP ABAP Environment without replicating that data into the ABAP system.

The blog demonstrates this using a Loyalty Management application.

Scenario

  • Customer loyalty information (Memberships) is stored in an external SAP HANA Cloud database.
  • Transaction data related to each membership is also stored in the same external database.
  • The business wants a Fiori application where users can:
    • Create loyalty memberships
    • Maintain loyalty transactions
    • Update and delete records
    • View accumulated loyalty points
    • Persist all changes directly in the external HANA database

 

Prerequisites:

  1. SAP BTP Sub-Account
  2. SAP HANA Cloud Instance
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment

Steps:

  1. Setup SAP HANA Cloud instance
  2. Create Schema and Tables
  3. Configure external connection to External HANA database from SAP BTP ABAP Environment using External Entities
  4. Create External Entities

Setup SAP HANA Cloud instance

Let’s start on with the creation of SAP HANA Cloud instance.

  1. Open SAP BTP cockpit and add the following Entitlements.


  2. Create HANA cloud subscription.


  3. Create HANA Cloud Instance


    Specify Instance name, Set DBADMIN password and choose Allow all IP address
    Spoiler
    Disclaimer on Database IP Access Configuration
    Allowing unrestricted access (e.g., permitting all IP addresses such as 0.0.0.0/0) to a database that contains production data introduces significant security risks. This approach should only be used in strictly controlled environments.


  4. Once the Instance status turns into Running state and click on Actions three dots and Open in SAP HANA Database Explorer. 
    Provide the DBADMIN credentials from the previous step to connect to the HANA database.


  5. Using Open SQL Console, perform the following steps for  Creating Remote Sources.
    Create a new remote source RS_LOYALTY using the SAP HANA database explorer, or SQL syntax. The parameters required vary by adapter. A remote source can be used to create virtual tables or to use the linked database feature.
CREATE REMOTE SOURCE "RS_LOYALTY"
	ADAPTER "hanaodbc"
		CONFIGURATION '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?><ConnectionProperties name="connectionproperties"><PropertyEntry name="adapterversion">1.0</PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="connectionmode">adapterproperties</PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="driver">libodbcHDB.so</PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="server"><url of HANA Cloud Instance>.hanacloud.ondemand.com</PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="port">443</PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="dml_mode">readwrite</PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="schema_alias"></PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="schema_alias_replacement"></PropertyEntry><PropertyEntry name="extraadapterproperties"></PropertyEntry></ConnectionProperties>'
			WITH CREDENTIAL TYPE 'PASSWORD' USING 'user=<db_user>;password=<db_user_password>;


Creation of PSE(Private Security Environment) / Certificate Collection

CREATE PSE RS_HANA;​


Get the DigiCert Global Root CA  from the DigiCert website https://cacerts.digicert.com/DigiCertGlobalRootCA.crt.pem .Create a certificate RS_CERT and insert the content of the downloaded certificate .

CREATE CERTIFICATE rs_cert FROM '
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
INSERT CONTENT OF THE CERTIFICATE FROM PREVIOUS STEP
-----END CERTIFICATE-----';


Add the certificate(s) created above to the certificate collection

ALTER PSE RS_HANA ADD CERTIFICATE rs_cert;

Set the purpose of the newly created PSE to REMOTE SOURCE

SET PSE RS_HANA PURPOSE REMOTE SOURCE;​

Create Schema and Tables

  • Create Schema
CREATE SCHEMA LOYALTY;​

  • Create Membership table: zlm_membship_ee

Stores membership details
Primary key: client + membershipuuid

create column table loyalty."zlm_membship_ee" (
"client" nvarchar (000003) default '000' not null, 
"membershipuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null , 
"membershipid" nvarchar (000010) default '0000000000' not null, 
"customer" nvarchar (000010) default '' not null, 
"createdby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"createdat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"locallastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null,
primary key ("client", "membershipuuid" )     
) column loadable
​
  • Create Transactions table: zlm_transctns_ee

Stores transactions linked to membership.
Contains transaction value and loyalty points.

create column table loyalty."zlm_transctns_ee" (
"client" nvarchar (000003) default '000' not null, 
"transactionuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null ,
"membershipuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null , 
"transactiondate" NVARCHAR (000008) DEFAULT '00000000' NOT NULL, 
"transactionvalue" DECIMAL (000010, 000002) DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL, 
"transactioncurrency" NVARCHAR (000005) DEFAULT '' NOT NULL, 
"loyaltypoints" BIGINT DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL, 
"createdby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"createdat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"locallastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null,
primary key ("client", "transactionuuid" )     
) column loadable

​


Configure connectivity to an external SAP HANA database from Steampunk for data access using External Entities

  1. Establish outbound SQL access using an external entity using ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse

    1. Define and activate a logical external schema whose configuration specifies the connection details for the outbound communication. Create a logical external schema in ABAP Development Tools (ADT) using the creation wizard.


    2.  Create an Outbound Service of the type Outbound SQL Access for your logical external schema.


    3. Create a Communication Scenario.


    4. Include the outbound service in the communication scenario.


  2. Configure External Connection
    In the Fiori Launchpad, Logon as Administrator and perform the following steps.
    1. Create a Communication Arrangement for scenario ZLM_COM_EXT_SCHEMA_EE.




    2.  Create a Communication System



      Toggle Remote SQL Access and choose Adapter Name as HANA (ODBC) 
      Adapter Configuration :Driver=libodbcHDB.so;ServerNode=*.hanacloud.ondemand.com(URL of the External Database):443;



    3. Create an Outbound User. Specify the HANA DB user which contains the necessary authorizations for accessing the External Schema.




Create CDS External Entities

Two types:
1. Writable External Entity → Used for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
2. Static External Entity → Used for SELECT (read only)

These entities map external tables to ABAP CDS.

In ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse: As an ABAP Developer, here’s what you need to do to establish outbound SQL access using a CDS external entity.

  • Define and activate an external entity that represents the external database object.As we are going to perform CUD operation on external database, we need to create Writable External Entity for table zlm_membship_ee which will be referred by external name zlm_membship_ee.
@EndUserText.label: 'Writable EE for HANA tab zlm_membship_ee'
define writable external entity zlm_membship_cud_ee external name "zlm_membship_ee"
{
  key client             : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key membershipuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      membershipid       : zlm_membershipid_ee           external name "membershipid";
      customer           : zlm_customer_ee               external name "customer";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby          : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat          : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby      : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat      : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided at runtime
​
​
  • We need to create Writable External Entity for table zlm_transctns_ee
@EndUserText.label: 'Writable EE for HANAtab zlm_transctns_ee'
define writable external entity zlm_transctns_cud_ee external name "zlm_transctns_ee"
{
  key client              : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key transactionuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "transactionuuid";
      membershipuuid      : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      transactiondate     : zlm_transactiondate_ee        external name "transactiondate";
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'transactioncurrency'
      transactionvalue    : zlm_transactionvalue_ee       external name "transactionvalue";
      transactioncurrency : zlm_transactioncurrency_ee    external name "transactioncurrency";
      loyaltypoints       : zlm_loyaltypoints_ee          external name "loyaltypoints";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby           : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat           : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby       : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided at runtime
​
​
  • To securely map a remote physical database object so it can be queried directly, we need to use STATIC EXTERNAL ENTITIES to fetch data from table zlm_membship_ee .
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Static EE for HANA tab zlm_membship_ee'
define external entity zlm_membship_ee external name "zlm_membship_ee"
{
  key client             : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key membershipuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      membershipid       : zlm_membershipid_ee           external name "membershipid";
      customer           : zlm_customer_ee               external name "customer";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby          : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat          : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby      : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat      : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided by zlm_ext_schema_ee
​
​
  • We need another STATIC EXTERNAL ENTITY for table zlm_transctns_ee
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Static EE for HANA tab zlm_transctns_ee'
define external entity zlm_transctns_ee external name "zlm_transctns_ee"
{
  key client              : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key transactionuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "transactionuuid";
      membershipuuid      : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      transactiondate     : zlm_transactiondate_ee        external name "transactiondate";
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'transactioncurrency'
      transactionvalue    : zlm_transactionvalue_ee       external name "transactionvalue";
      transactioncurrency : zlm_transactioncurrency_ee    external name "transactioncurrency";
      loyaltypoints       : zlm_loyaltypoints_ee          external name "loyaltypoints";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby           : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat           : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby       : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided by zlm_ext_schema_ee

​

Key Takeaways.

External entities enable real-time integration with external databases without replication.

In Part 2 of this series, we will build a RAP application using the data model created here.
